perturbation.original_text: NO materials or documents which have been furnished by Submitting Party to Receiving Party will be returned, but instead will be disposed of in an appropriate manner to maintain confidentiality.
perturbation.changed_text: NO materials or documents which have been furnished by Submitting Party to Receiving Party will be returned, and will be disposed of.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of 'in an appropriate manner to maintain confidentiality' eliminates the explicit requirement to handle the materials in a way that protects the Submitting Party's confidentiality. This could lead to negligent handling of confidential information and potentially violate California's trade secret protection laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. By removing the qualifier that disposal must be done in a manner to maintain confidentiality, the contract weakens the protection afforded to trade secrets and may violate the requirement to take reasonable measures.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The original text requires disposal of materials in an "appropriate manner to maintain confidentiality." The changed text removes the "to maintain confidentiality" clause. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both indicate that this removal weakens trade secret protection. Snippet 1 defines 'improper means' to include breach of a duty to maintain secrecy (CIV 3426.1). By removing the explicit confidentiality requirement, the changed contract language increases the risk of trade secret misappropriation, thereby creating a legal contradiction by potentially violating the duty to maintain secrecy as required by CUTSA and defined in California Civil Code 3426.1.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall not through operations of succession or otherwise place Confidential Information in the hands of a competitor of Submitting Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall not through operations of succession place Confidential Information in the hands of a competitor of Submitting Party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'or otherwise', the contract creates a loophole that allows the Receiving Party to share confidential information with a competitor through means other than succession (e.g., a direct sale of the information). This weakens the protection of trade secrets and conflicts with California law.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA aims to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets. Removing 'or otherwise' creates a loophole enabling misappropriation by means other than 'succession', potentially violating the requirement to protect trade secrets through reasonable measures.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The omission of "or otherwise" weakens the original contract's protection of confidential information. The original text prevents the Receiving Party from placing Confidential Information in the hands of a competitor through succession or other means. The changed text only prohibits this through succession, creating a loophole. This directly contradicts the intent of CUTSA, as explained by the LLM, which is to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines "Misappropriation" in CIV 3426.1(b) as the "Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent". The modified contract now allows disclosure through means other than succession, which potentially constitutes misappropriation, creating a direct legal contradiction. The 'or otherwise' was clearly meant to cast a wider protective net than merely succession, and its removal severely weakens that protection.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party agrees that it will take all reasonable steps to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure or use of Confidential Information in order to prevent the Confidential Information from falling into the public domain or the possession of unauthorized persons.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party agrees that it will take steps to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure or use of Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'all reasonable' and 'in order to prevent the Confidential Information from falling into the public domain or the possession of unauthorized persons' weakens the Receiving Party's obligation to protect the Submitting Party's confidential information. This makes it more difficult to enforce the confidentiality agreement and may violate California's trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires taking reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. Removing 'all reasonable' directly contradicts this standard, as it allows for less stringent protection measures and increased risk of disclosure, violating the law.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The original text mandated "all reasonable steps" to protect confidential information to prevent its disclosure. The changed text removes "all reasonable" and "in order to prevent the Confidential Information from falling into the public domain or the possession of unauthorized persons", weakening the obligation. The Law Explanation states that CUTSA requires taking reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Civil Code 3426.1, defines 'improper means' to include breach of a duty to maintain secrecy. Removing the 'all reasonable' standard and the explicit purpose of preventing disclosure contradicts the requirement to take reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed under the laws of the State of New York without regard to principles of conflict of laws of such state. The federal and state courts within the State of New York shall have exclusive jurisdiction to adjudicate any dispute arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of New York. The federal and state courts within the State of New York shall have jurisdiction to adjudicate any dispute arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'without regard to principles of conflict of laws of such state' and 'exclusive' changes the jurisdiction clause. Without the 'conflict of laws' clause, a California court might apply NY law even if CA law is more appropriate. Removing 'exclusive' allows disputes to be litigated outside of New York, potentially denying the Submitting Party the protections that were originally intended and agreed upon and perhaps required under California law if the Submitting Party is located and operating in California. While the agreement is written in New York, Mom Inventors, Inc. are located in California, therefore California law may apply.
perturbation.law_explanation: CCP 410.10 grants California courts jurisdiction over any cause. By removing the 'exclusive' nature of New York Jurisdiction, disputes may arise whether a New York court needs to make a ruling based on this, or if a California court can also make judgements. This could result in the submitting party having to follow the decisions in New York and California courts, potentially creating contradictory legal problems.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. JURISDICTION AND SERVICE OF PROCESS [410.10 - 418.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) CHAPTER 1. Jurisdiction and Forum [410.10 - 410.70] ( Heading of Chapter 1 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 3. ) ARTICLE 1. Jurisdiction [410.10- 410.10.] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) 410.10. A court of this state may exercise jurisdiction on any basis not inconsistent with the Constitution of this state or of the United States. (Added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610.)
perturbation.justification: The omission of 'exclusive' from the jurisdiction clause, coupled with the removal of 'without regard to principles of conflict of laws of such state,' creates a legal contradiction because it opens the door for litigation outside of New York. CCP 410.10 states that 'A court of this state may exercise jurisdiction on any basis not inconsistent with the Constitution of this state or of the United States,' implying that California courts could assert jurisdiction. By removing 'exclusive', the modified contract text directly contradicts the original intent of limiting jurisdiction to New York, which could lead to the application of different state laws and potentially conflicting judgments, weakening the original protection. This aligns with the definition of Omission Legal Contradiction, where essential information is removed, creating legal loopholes.
